---
title: "Preface"
---

**ferx** is a fast, ergonomic R package for nonlinear mixed effects (NLME) modeling, powered by a Rust backend ([ferx-core](https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-core)).
It targets the pharmacokineticist who wants NONMEM-like expressiveness with modern tooling: a plain-text model DSL, tidy R output, and a solver fast enough to iterate quickly.

## What this book covers

This book walks through ferx from first install to advanced modeling techniques.
It is organized into three parts:

**Getting started** covers installation (including the optional Enzyme autodiff backend) and a complete quickstart example.

**Core workflow** goes deep on the model DSL, fitting options, diagnostic output, and simulation/VPC — the four things you do on every project.

**Advanced topics** covers parameter transforms, ODE models, BLOQ/M3 censoring, and inter-occasion variability.

Every code chunk in this book is executed on CI and the output shown is real.
Where fits are slow, results are cached with `cache: true`; you can always clear the cache and re-run.

## Prerequisites

- R ≥ 4.2
- Rust toolchain (see @sec-installation)
- Basic familiarity with population PK/PD modeling (one-compartment models, ETAs, RUV)

## Companion resources

- [ferx-r API reference](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/reference/) — function-level docs
- [Model DSL reference](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/model-dsl/overview.html) — complete DSL syntax
- [ferx-core internals](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-core/overview.html) — the Rust engine
- [GitHub](https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r) — source and issue tracker
